title: Biometrics survey — FMR/FNMR, identification vs verification, cost-benefit
html:
TL;DR

The page of the Jain et al. survey where the math gets serious: FMR scales linearly with database size when you go from verification to identification. A 0.001% verification False Match Rate becomes a 10% identification FMR at 10,000 users — a hacker with all ten fingers gets in. This is why "let's just put biometrics on everything" doesn't survive contact with arithmetic.

Also includes:

  • The Gartner/Forrester quote Yahir would cite to anyone ever again: 20–50% of all help-desk calls are password resets, and the average reset costs $38. That's the cost-benefit case for biometrics in a corporate login system.
  • The airport-no-fly-list worked example — 200 false alarms a day from a 100-person watchlist over a 200,000-person daily traffic.
  • The argument for multimodal biometrics as the only viable path for large-N identification.

Why it's in the graph

The $38/reset stat is one of those numbers Yahir will want at his fingertips for any cold-email or pitch involving passwordless auth, SSO, or identity products. Save it as a fact-citation, not just a vibe.

The FMR-blows-up math is the kind of "obvious in hindsight, devastating in the room" insight worth keeping near asking the right question — it's a clean instance of the wrong question makes the answer worthless. Verification accuracy and identification accuracy are not the same thing, and conflating them is how vendors sell broken systems.

What's actually on the page

  • fmr fnmr tradeoff with the worked numbers: 5-digit PIN equivalence at 0.001% FMR, password-reset cost-benefit, brute-force economics.
  • identification vs verification — N×FMR scaling, why fast hardware doesn't save identification accuracy.
  • multimodal biometrics as the only large-scale fix — face+fingerprint, multi-finger, etc.
  • negative recognition in airports/forensics — semi-automatic mode where the system narrows and humans decide. Civil-liberties argument: if you're not in the criminal database, the system doesn't remember you.
  • AFIS (Automatic Fingerprint Identification System) — narrows millions to hundreds for a human expert.

The number Yahir should remember

20–50% of all help-desk calls are password resets. Average labor cost: ~$38/reset. (Gartner / Forrester, vintage but directionally still right.)

Connections

  • The "semi-automatic" framing — system filters, human decides — is the same shape as how Yahir thinks about ai search optimization and the ai visibility engine: the AI narrows, the human (or the agency client) closes. Worth a future canvas connecting negative recognition and AI-narrowed lead lists.
  • The Gartner stat is also evidence for any bridge income strategies play that involves selling to mid-market IT.
